1: The oracle-reception formula again — but the message of chapter 7 is qualitatively different from everything before. This chapter is a sustained lament over finality, with a distinctive elevated/poetic style that breaks from Ezekiel's usual prose. The shift in register signals the gravity of what follows.
2: qets ('the end'): the word Amos used for the northern kingdom's fate (Amos 8:2) is now applied to the southern. Qets denotes a terminal point — not merely a bad period but the cessation of something. arba kanfot ha'aretz ('four corners of the land'): totalistic formula — every direction, nothing excluded. The end comes from all quadrants simultaneously.
3: shafachti appi ('I will pour out my wrath upon you'): aph is both 'nose' and 'wrath' in Hebrew — the image of flaring nostrils as the original somatic register of anger. The pouring metaphor suggests wrath as accumulated liquid finally released, uncontainable and unstoppable. mishpatim ('judgments'): the deeds that fill the "end" are the divine verdicts — YHWH's legal actions against covenant breach.
4: lo-tachon einecha ('my eye will not spare you'): the verb chus (to have compassion, spare) is used negatively here — the divine compassion that normally withholds judgment is explicitly withdrawn. Ezekiel 8:18 and 9:5 repeat this formula. The removal of divine mercy is itself a judicial act, not arbitrary cruelty but the withdrawal of a staying grace that was repeatedly refused.
5: ra'ah achat ra'ah ('a disaster, a singular disaster'): the repetition of the word creates an ominous emphasis — 'a disaster, one disaster.' The LXX reads kakía mía kakía. This singular unique disaster will be unlike the repeated warnings and partial judgments that preceded it. This is the final act.
6: heqitz elayich ('the end has awakened against you'): qetz (end) is personified as a sleeping predator now roused. The verb huqitz (Hiphil of quts) means 'to awaken' — judgment was dormant, patient; now it stirs. The wordplay qetz/qits (end/summer) echoes Amos 8:2's famous vision.
7: hatsefira ba'ah ('the dawn has come, the doom has come'): tsefira is rare — possibly 'morning' or 'crown/garland' (hapax in this sense) — pointing to an ironic dawning not of light but of doom. The day dawns but brings darkness. lo-har hechim ('not a joyful shout on the mountains'): the mountains that should echo with the joyful shout of harvest (Isa 16:10) will be silenced.
8: qerov lishpoch chamati ('soon I will pour out my fury upon you'): chamath (fury, heat of anger) — a stronger term than aph — denotes the burning intensity of the divine anger that has been holding back. The second person singular address makes this intensely personal: YHWH speaks directly to the land/people as if face to face.
9: lo-tachus einecha velo echmol ('my eye will not spare and I will not pity'): the doubled refusal of mercy — both the visual mode of compassion (chus b'ayin, spare with the eye) and the emotional compassion (chamal, pity/have mercy) are withdrawn. The yeda'tem formula again: judgment serves revelation.
10: mateh parach hazadon tsats ('the rod has blossomed, pride has budded'): unusual agricultural metaphor. The 'rod' is likely the rod of punishment/affliction that has now 'blossomed' — it has come to full growth and is ready to execute. Zadon (pride/arrogance): the bloom of Israel's arrogance has triggered the bloom of its judgment. Nature's growth cycle becomes the metaphor for moral consequence.
11: hachamas qam lemateh resha ('violence has grown into a rod of wickedness'): the violence that filled the land has become the instrument of its own destruction — a rod that punishes. This theme of sin becoming the agent of its own punishment is central to prophetic theology. Lo-meylahem ('nothing of their wealth') — no remainder survives.
12: ba ha'et higi'a hayom ('the time has come, the day has arrived'): the prophetic 'day of YHWH' language — a day that has been anticipated is now arriving. ha-mocher al-yismach ('let not the buyer rejoice'): commercial transactions will be meaningless — the land itself is forfeited, and buying property is pointless when everything is about to be seized by the invader.
13: ki-hazon el-kol-hamonah lo yashuv ('for the vision concerning its whole multitude will not turn back'): the divine vision-word is irrevocable — once spoken and set in motion, it cannot be recalled or softened. The prophetic word in Ezekiel functions as divine performative speech: it brings into existence what it declares.
14: taq'u vataqoa ('they blow the trumpet'): the war trumpet signals mobilization, but no one goes to battle — the will to fight has been divinely removed. The trumpet sounds into an emptiness that reveals the completeness of judgment: no military response is possible because YHWH's wrath has paralyzed the defenders.
15: The three-fold judgment of sword, pestilence, and famine again (cf. 6:12), but here applied to specific locations: sword in the field, pestilence and famine within the city. The geography of doom is complete — there is no safe quarter. The siege creates an inner circle of starvation while the sword awaits those who try to escape.
16: umiltetu vehayu ('and those who escape, they will be'): even the escapees are covered in divine judgment — they will be on the mountains 'like doves of the valleys' (col-yon ha'amaqim), each mourning alone. The comparison to solitary mourning doves captures the total breakdown of community: isolated, grieving, without purpose.
17: kol-yadayim tirpenah ('all hands shall go limp'): the paralysis of terror — the inability to act, fight, or flee. The idiom raphah yadayim (limp/feeble hands) recurs in Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Zephaniah as the somatic register of total demoralization. berka'ayim telaknah mayim ('knees run with water'): the physical sign of extreme fear, incontinence as the body's ultimate surrender.
18: chagor saq ('gird themselves with sackcloth'): the mourning rite of sackcloth — worn against the skin as a sign of grief and abasement. ufachad yechasse ('terror will cover them'): pachad (dread, terror) — the paralyzing fear that removes the capacity for resistance. vel-kol-panim bushah ('on all faces shame'): universal shame as the condition of the defeated.
19: kashpam batchutzot yashlichu ('their silver they will throw into the streets'): the ultimate economic collapse — wealth becomes worthless. kesef niddah yihyeh ('their silver will be like a rejected thing'): niddah (menstrual impurity) — silver described as ritually unclean and untouchable, the ultimate devaluation. beyom evrat YHWH ('on the day of YHWH's wrath'): the first explicit use of the 'day of YHWH' formula in Ezekiel.
20: tzvi adyo lega'on samu ('the beauty of his ornaments — they set it for pride'): Israel's beautiful temple treasures (the tzvi, glory/beauty) were used to make idols. The divine gift of beauty — architecture, sacred objects, wealth — was perverted into the material of false worship. The very blessings became the instruments of the betrayal.
21: unatativ byad hazarim levalah ('I will give it as plunder to strangers to desecrate'): YHWH actively hands Israel's treasured things to pagan desecrators — not as abandonment but as a deliberate act of judgment through the agency of enemies. The 'most wicked of the nations' as YHWH's instruments echoes the Habakkuk theodicy: how can YHWH use the unrighteous to judge his people?
22: utsefuni lo-ye'asem ('my treasured place shall be profaned'): tsafun (that which is hidden/treasured/secret) — the innermost holy space, the sanctuary. YHWH turns his face away, allowing the profaners to enter and desecrate what he has until now protected. The divine withdrawal of protection makes the violence possible.
23: asoh harattok ('make the chain'): a command to Ezekiel to forge a chain — a sign act of coming captivity. The land is full of bloodshed (mishpat damim) and the city full of violence (chamas). The chain anticipates the exile: Israel will be led away bound.
24: miqdasheihem yechullalu ('their sanctuaries shall be desecrated'): plural miqdashim — all the holy places, not just Jerusalem. The word challal (desecrate/profane) is from the root meaning 'pierced/opened' — to violate what was sealed and protected. The divine protection of the holy place is withdrawn, and the sacred is exposed to the profane.
25: tavo charchurah ('anguish comes'): charcharah (trembling/anguish) — a rare term that combines physical and emotional distress. They will seek peace (shalom) but there will be none — the divine gift of shalom has been permanently interrupted. The false prophets' cry of 'peace, peace' (13:10) is answered by this declared absence of peace.
26: chazon me'at yavoh ('calamity upon calamity shall come, and vision will follow vision'): in a savage irony, the moment judgment falls is when visions multiply — but they are visions of doom, not salvation. Torah mekohen tovad ('instruction from the priest is lost'): the three channels of divine communication — vision (chazon from the prophet), instruction (Torah from the priest), counsel (etsah from the elder) — all simultaneously fail. The complete collapse of covenant mediation.
27: hamelech yitabel ('the king will mourn'): the king himself becomes a mourner — dressed in garments of mourning rather than royal robes. The total inversion of the social order: the priest loses Torah, the elder loses counsel, the king loses power, the people lose security. yadayim am ha'aretz tirpenah ('the hands of the people of the land will go limp'): the paralyzing terror now extends through all social strata. Divine judgment equalizes everyone in helplessness.
